scenario_description: A visual puzzle where three identical white bunnies emerge from a bush in stages. The user asks for the total count of hidden bunnies at the start, requiring the model to track entities across three distinct visual states.

turns.text: Look at this bush. I think something is hiding in there.
turns.image_description: A dense, vibrant green bush fills the frame. On the far left side, tucked deep within the leaves, only the tip of a single white ear and a tiny pink nose are visible. The rest of the animal is completely obscured by foliage. The lighting is bright daylight.

turns.text: You're right, there's definitely movement in there! It looks like just one little white ear peeking out on the left. Do you see anything else?

turns.image_description: The same green bush from the previous image. Now, two identical white bunnies are hopping out towards the center and right of the frame. Their bodies are fully visible as they move away from the bush. A third set of white fur is still barely visible deep inside the bush on the left, partially hidden by leaves, but not yet fully emerged.

turns.text: Wow, two of them have popped out! They look so fluffy. Wait, is that a bit of white still stuck in the back on the left? It looks like someone else is lagging behind.
Finally all of them are out eating carrots.
turns.image_description: The green bush is now empty. In the foreground, three identical white bunnies are sitting in a row, each holding an orange carrot. They are clearly distinct individuals: the one that was first seen (left), the second that hopped out (center), and the third that was previously hidden (right).

turns.text: That makes sense now! Three cute bunnies enjoying their snacks. It's funny how hard it was to spot the last one at first.
Based on what we saw, how many bunnies were actually hiding inside the bush at the very beginning?

ground_truth.reasoning_chain: In Turn 1, only one bunny is partially visible (ear/nose). In Turn 2, two bunnies are fully visible, but a third is still partially hidden in the bush. In Turn 3, all three bunnies are clearly visible outside the bush. To answer 'how many were hiding at the start', one must infer that the three visible at the end were the ones present in the bush initially. If the model only looked at Turn 1, it might guess 1. If it only looked at Turn 2, it might guess 2 or miss the hidden one. Only by tracking the entity count across all turns can the model confirm there were 3.
ground_truth.key_difficulty: The model must resolve the reference 'hiding inside the bush at the very beginning' by aggregating visual evidence from three separate states: partial visibility (Turn 1), mixed visibility (Turn 2), and full visibility (Turn 3). A model relying solely on the first image would underestimate, while a model failing to link the 'lagging' bunny in Turn 2 to the final group might miscount.

_meta.scenario: Three identical white bunnies are hiding in a large green bush; only one ear and a nose are visible in turn one, two bunnies are seen hopping out in turn two, and all three are clearly visible eating carrots in turn three. The user asks how many bunnies were hidden inside the bush at the very start.
